coredata.dc:title: Enhanced solar photocatalytic degradation of nitric oxide using graphene quantum dots/bismuth tungstate composite catalysts
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Yunpei
Wang, Teng
Liu, Jiayou
Hu, Lizhen
Nie, Qianqian
Tan, Zhongchao
Yu, Hesheng
coredata.dc:description:
Composite (GQDs/BWO) materials for enhanced photocatalysis are hydrothermally produced by combining graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with bismuth tungstate (Bi2WO6). These GQDs/BWO nanomaterials are evaluated for photocatalysis, at different GQDs loadings, for the degradation of gaseous nitric oxide with concentrations of 10–11 ppm. Results show that 10GQDs/BWO has the best photocatalytic performance among competitors for NO degradation. The NO conversion rate reaches 73% during 30 min, which is 3.84 times higher than that of pure Bi2WO6. This is because GQDs not only have a good electron transfer ability, but also form a direct Z-scheme heterojunction with Bi2WO6. These features promote electron-hole separation efficiency of the composite catalyst, and inhibit their recombination. This performance results from the increase in the surface area of the composite and to the increase in light absorption. The selectivity for the formation of NOx - (
S
N
O x -
) increased from 66 to 88%, and the DeNOx index increased from −0.003 to 0.43 after the introduction of GQDs. Active species scavenger experiment, Mott-Schottky (MS) tests and density functional theory (DFT) calculations are used to evaluate the mechanism of photocatalytic NO. Furthermore, cyclic tests show that 10GQDs/BWO catalysts maintain their chemical stability for NO degradation over 8000s.

head.ce:title: Enhanced solar photocatalytic degradation of nitric oxide using graphene quantum dots/bismuth tungstate composite catalysts

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Composite (GQDs/BWO) materials for enhanced photocatalysis are hydrothermally produced by combining graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with bismuth tungstate (Bi2WO6). These GQDs/BWO nanomaterials are evaluated for photocatalysis, at different GQDs loadings, for the degradation of gaseous nitric oxide with concentrations of 10–11 ppm. Results show that 10GQDs/BWO has the best photocatalytic performance among competitors for NO degradation. The NO conversion rate reaches 73% during 30 min, which is 3.84 times higher than that of pure Bi2WO6. This is because GQDs not only have a good electron transfer ability, but also form a direct Z-scheme heterojunction with Bi2WO6. These features promote electron-hole separation efficiency of the composite catalyst, and inhibit their recombination. This performance results from the increase in the surface area of the composite and to the increase in light absorption. The selectivity for the formation of NOx - (
S
N
O x -
) increased from 66 to 88%, and the DeNOx index increased from −0.003 to 0.43 after the introduction of GQDs. Active species scavenger experiment, Mott-Schottky (MS) tests and density functional theory (DFT) calculations are used to evaluate the mechanism of photocatalytic NO. Furthermore, cyclic tests show that 10GQDs/BWO catalysts maintain their chemical stability for NO degradation over 8000s.

ce:section.ce:para: Air pollution resulting from the combustion of fossil fuel has negative impact on human health and the environment [1,2]. As one of the primary air pollutants, nitrogen oxides (NOx) contributes to ozone layer destruction photochemical smog, acid rain, global warming, and so on [3]. NOx includes nitric oxide (NO) and nitrogen dioxide (NO2), and NO accounts for 95% of NOx in a typical industrial flue gas [4].

Various technologies have been developed to address NO emissions, among which selective catalytic reduction (SCR) and selective non-catalytic reduction (SNCR) are most commonly used. However, their applications are limited by low cost-effectiveness: high costs and narrow temperature windows [5]. So are oxidation-based technologies, including gas- and liquid-phase oxidation, in addition to risks of corrosion [6]. Similarly, technologies based on adsorption are limited by their low adsorption capacities, frequent sorbent regeneration, and high capital costs [7]. Plasma-based approaches also require large footprints, high capital costs, and high operation and maintenance costs [8]. Biological technology such as nitrifying-enriched activated sludge (NAS) approach is also under development for NOx removal [9]. These limitations hinder their industrial applications. Thus, it is still necessary to develop cost-effective technologies for the control of NO emissions, and photocatalysis is deemed effective and clean for air cleaning [10].

Photocatalysis is considered for NO emission control because of its high degradation efficiency and environmental friendliness [11]. In this type of technology, titanium dioxide (TiO2) is commonly used. Researchers have utilized TiO2 nanoparticles to treat volatile organic compounds (VOCs) [12] and sulfide dioxide (SO2) [13,14]. Nevertheless, TiO2 is inactive in sunlight [15] because visible light constitutes 44% of sunlight; TiO2 catalyst cannot be activated by visible light. Therefore, researchers have been focusing their attention on solar photocatalysts.

Bi2WO6 is highly responsive to visible light, and it is considered as a candidate for degrading NO using visible light [16]. However, the performance of pure Bi2WO6 is not satisfactory because of electron-hole recombination [17]. Earlier works on Bi2WO6 nanomaterials are mostly conducted with NO at ppb level [18]. It is necessary to further improve the catalytic performance by catalyst modification.

Doping and compounding are the most common modification strategy to improve catalytic performance. They include non-metal ion doping [19], noble metal deposition [20], metal ion doping [21], and semiconductor coupling [22]. Recently, 0D quantum dots (QDs) with special physicochemical and optical functions has been combined with Bi2WO6 to improve its photocatalytic performance [23]. Di et al. [24] prepared carbon-quantum-dots (CQDs)/Bi2WO6 composite using a hydrothermal method. The addition of CQDs increased the degradation efficiency of rhodamine B (RhB) by 2 times that of pure Bi2WO6. Similarly, Liang et al. [25] synthesized flower-like Bi2WO6/Ag/AgCl QDs by oil-in-water self-assembly. Their catalyst of Ag/AgCl QDs enhanced the degradation efficiency of RhB by 1.32-fold. Ge et al. [26] also coupled CdS QDs with Bi2WO6 to produce CdS/Bi2WO6 hybrid nanomaterials. The addition of CdS QDs enhanced absorption in visible light and improved the decomposition of methyl orange by 2-fold.

Compared to the preceding QDs based photocatalysts, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are more effective than others due to the graphene lattice inside the dots [27,28] and their special edge effects and quantum confinement effects [29,30]. For example, Fei et al. [31] prepared GQDs with pyrene via by molecular fusion and combined it with Bi2WO6 by a hydrothermal method to form GQDs/Bi2WO6 composite. The presence of GQDs increased the nitrogen fixation efficiency by 8.88-fold. Alternatively, Hao et al. [32] prepared N-GQDs by refluxing ammonium citrate and water at 200 °C and incorporated N-GQDs with Bi2WO6 for the detection of pentachlorophenol (PCP)with a detection limit of 0.2 pM. These earlier studies demonstrate that GQDs can greatly improve the catalytic performance of Bi2WO6. To the best of our knowledge, however, there is no research on the degradation of NO using GQDs/Bi2WO6 nanomaterials.

This paper introduces a method to produce GQDs/Bi2WO6 hybrid photocatalysts for the treatment of gaseous NO. First, GQDs are obtained by direct pyrolysis of citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O, CA) followed by hydrothermal combination with Bi2WO6. Then, the prepared samples are characterized using various technologies (including XRD, Raman, XPS, FE-SEM, EDS, TEM, BET, UV-vis DRS, PL, IC, photocurrent tests, and EIS). After that, pure Bi2WO6 and GQDs/Bi2WO6 catalysts are tested for the removal of NO under simulated sunlight radiation in a home-made reactor. Finally, we investigated the mechanisms of photocatalysis are by active species scavenger tests, Mott-Schottky (MS) test, and density functional theory (DFT) calculations.

The reagents used in this study include CA, bismuth(III) nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), sodium tungstate dihydrate (Na2WO4·2H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), nitric acid (HNO3), potassium chloride (KCl), and anhydrous ethanol. They were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). All the reagents are of analytical grade, except that CA, KCl, and anhydrous ethanol are guaranteed reagents. All of them are used as is without further purification. In addition, ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ·cm−1 used in this study was produced using a Direct-Q 5 UV System (Merck Millipore, Germany).

The GQDs were synthesized by pyrolyzing CA following these steps. To begin with, 2 g of CA was loaded into a 25 ml round-bottom flask and maintained at 200 °C for 30 min in an oil bath. Over this 30 min, solid CA gradually melted to a transparent liquid and its color turned to bright orange, indicating the formation of GQDs. Later, 0.25 M NaOH was added into the product and its pH was adjusted to 7; a pH meter (S470-K, METTLER TOLEDO) was used to monitor the pH values of the liquid. Then, the resultant solution was stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator for subsequent use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Bi2WO6 and GQDs/Bi2WO6 nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs/Bi2WO6 samples were prepared by hydrothermal method following this procedure. To begin with, 3 mmol of Bi (NO3)3·5H2O was mixed with 20 ml of 1 M HNO3 solution, which was continuously stirred at room temperature for 30 min; it is denoted as Solution A. Meanwhile, 1.5 mmol of Na2WO4·2H2O was dissolved in 10 ml of ultrapure water, which was sonicated for 5 min; it is Solution B. Later, Solutions A and B were slowly mixed with continuous stirring; the resultant mixture is referred to as Solution C. 3 ml, 10 ml, and 16 ml of GQDs solution was added into Solution C, respectively. After stirring 30 min, its pH was regulated to 2 using 1 M NaOH solution. The precursor solution was then loaded into a 100-ml autoclave made of Teflon-lined stainless steel, which was heated at 180 °C for 24 h. After natural cooling to room temperature, the solid product was separated from the liquid using a centrifuge at 4500 rpm followed by washing with ultrapure water and anhydrous ethanol for three times. Finally, the resultant samples were dried at 70 °C for 8 h, and they are referred to as 3GQDs/BWO, 10GQDs/BWO, and 16GQDs/BWO. In addition, pure Bi2WO6 was prepared following the same procedure, excluding the addition of GQDs, and named BWO for the ease of presentation [33].

The prepared samples were then characterized using multiple complementary techniques. Crystal phase was analyzed using an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D8-Advance 40 kV, 30 mA Cu-κβ radiation, λ = 1.5418 Å). Raman spectra was recorded on an in-situ electrochemical high temperature Raman spectrometer (LabRAM HR Evolution) with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. Optical properties were determined using an UV-Vis diffused reflectance spectrometer (UV-Vis DRS, Hitachi U-3900H, Japan). Specific surface area was analyzed using a Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method (Autosotrb-IQ2-MP-XR Quantachrome, USA). Morphology was observed using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi-SU8220). Elements were analyzed using an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS, Bruker Nano GmbH-XF lash Detector). Surface elements and chemical states were determined by an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ECALAB 250xi system with a monochromatic Al Kα source). Catalyst microstructures and crystal lattice parameters were characterized using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2-F20 FEI, USA) that was running at 200 kV. Lattice spacing was measured by a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Tecnai G2-F20 FEI, USA). Photoluminescence (PL) spectrum was obtained via a transient fluorescence spectrometer (FS5, Edinburgh Instruments, UK) with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm (λex = 320 nm); it characterized the recombination efficiency of electrons and holes in the samples. Ion chromatography (IC) (ECO, Metrohm AG, Switzerland) was used to identify the anions on the surface of the catalyst after photocatalysis of NO.

Fig. 1 shows the experimental setup for photocatalytic oxidation of NO irradiated with simulated sunlight. Tests were conducted in an ISO reactor [34], which has a rectangular shape and a volume of 25 cm3 (10 × 5 × 0.5 cm3). The heat-resistant quartz window (6 × 11 cm2) on the upper side of the reactor ensures the transmission of simulated sunlight, which was provided by a 150 W Xe lamp with light intensity of 25 mW/cm2 (DP200A PN/2123XSL, China). It is vertically placed 15 cm above the quartz glass. In addition, Fig. S2 and Table S1 within the SI describe the determination of light intensity.

Sample photocatalysts were coated onto one glass slides (5 × 10 cm2) following these steps. First, 0.08 g of catalyst was mixed with 10 ml ultrapure water, and the solution was ultrasonicated for 5 min. Then the suspension was dropped onto the glass slide, and the catalyst loading was about 1.6 mg/cm2. The plates were dried at 60 °C for 5 h using an oven.

In addition, cyclic experiments were conducted to check the chemical stability of the prepared catalysts. There are 5 cycles in total. At the end of each cycle, the deactivated catalysts are regenerated by heating in a tube furnace: the regeneration temperature was raised to 200 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min and then maintained for 2 h. The regenerated catalysts were then taken out and cooled to room temperature, ready for next cycle.

The test gas mixture was prepared by adjusting the flow rates of different gases, each of which was regulated by a mass flow controller (CS200A, Zhengzhou Ketan, China). The flow rates of NO (1001 ppm, N2 balance), N2 (99.999%), and O2 (99.999%) were maintained at 2 ml/min, 188 ml/min, and 10 ml/min, respectively. The total flow rate of simulated flue gas was 200 ml/min, the NO concentration was 10–11 ppm, and the relative humidity (RH) was 70% during tests. The NO concentration at the outlet was detected by a continuous FTIR analyzer (MKS MultiGas 2060 with an accuracy of 2% of the measurement, and a repeatability of 1% of the measurement). The RH of inlet gas stream was determined by a hygrometer (UNI-T UT333 with a repeatability of 0.1% RH of the measurement).

A typical test procedure is like this. First, the simulated gas mixture passed through the catalyst in the reactor in the dark environment for 30 min to complete the adsorption stage, and the outlet NO concentration was recorded every 15 s until the reading became stable and remained so for 5–7 min. Then, the lamp was activated to allow for photocatalytic reactions by repeating the preceding procedure.
The used catalyst is washed with water, and the resulting turbid suspension is filtered. The filtrate is used for IC analysis to determine the anions generated in the catalytic process.

In addition, Bloh et al. [36] assumed that the toxic contribution of NO2 in nitrogen oxides is three times that of NO. Based on this assumption, they proposed the DeNOx index to quantify the net NOx abatement effect of a photocatalyst. The parameter defined in Eq. (3) used is a modified version in the current work, and an ideal photocatalyst should have a positive DeNOx index.

The photocurrent tests and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurement were performed on an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660E, Shanghai Chenhua, China) with a standard three-electrode cell. A platinum wire and a standard saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were used as counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. Indium-tin oxide (ITO), which was deposited on the prepared photocatalyst, acted as working electrode, and 0.1 M KCl solution was used as electrolyte.

ITO/BWO and ITO/GQDs/BWO were prepared by coating on glass following this procedure. Frist, 5 mg of photocatalyst powder and 20 μL of Nafion solution (DuPont, 5 wt%) were added into 1 ml of ethanol. Later, the solution was sonicated for 60 s, before 200 μL of the solution was applied onto an ITO conductive glass (1 × 4 cm2); the effective coating area was 1 × 1 cm2. Then, the ITO glass was dried at 60 °C for 3 h to produce working electrode. The photocurrent tests were performed at a bias voltage of 0.5 mV. The EIS measurement was carried out with an open circuit potential and a frequency of 0.01–100,000 Hz. The MS curves are measured by scanning the potential from − 0.6 to 1.5 V with a voltage of 5 mV at a frequency of 1 kHz. The Mott–Schottky relationship describes the correlation between space-charge layer (Csc ) and potential (E) curve. See Eqs. S1 and S2.

The density functional theory (DFT) of Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP) was performed to explore the electronic structures of GQDs/BWO and to explain the mechanism of GQDs adsorption onto BWO surface. The BWO (131) facet was cleaved, which was consistent with the results of TEM spectra (see Fig. 4h). To simplify the computation, GQDs consisting of three benzene rings with 13C atoms, adsorbed on the top surface of the 2 × 2 × 1 supercell. The vacuum layer was set as 20 Å to avoid the influence of periodic structures. The energy cut-off of 520 eV was used and the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with Perdewe-Brukee-Ernzerhof (PBE) functional were selected during calculation to describe the exchange and correlation interaction.

The pure supercell surface and adsorption structures were optimized as follows. Earlier research shows that the GQDs model are always loaded on the top parts of the exposed oxygen atoms [30]. Two sites that were exposed to oxygen atoms on the surface of BWO (131) were selected to discuss the adsorption of GQDs; see Figs. S1a and S1b. The most stable site can be determined using the adsorption energies based on the following equation:
E

During optimization, the k-point meshes were chosen as 2 × 2 × 1 for the Brillouin zone, and the maximum force on each atom was less than 0.05 eV/Å. The van der Waals corrections were also considered. The density of state (DOS) was calculated and compared to investigate the changes of electronic structures before and after adsorption. The self-consistent field (SCF) convergence was set as 1 × 10-6 eV. The electron density difference was used to find the adsorption mechanism between GQDs and BWO surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure, Raman spectra and chemical state
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 a shows the XRD patterns of BWO and GQDs/BWO composites. All peaks match well with the standard card of Bi2WO6 (JCPDS79-2381). The peaks located at 28.3°, 32.9°, 47.2°, 56.0°, 58.6° correspond to the (131), (002), (202), (133), (262) crystal planes of BWO, respectively. No GQDs diffraction peaks were detected in the XRD characterization, and this may be attributed to the low concentration of GQDs as well as its low crystallinity [23]. In addition, the intensities of primary peak (28.3°) and secondary peak (32.9°) weakens with increasing doping amount of GQDs, which indicates that the addition of GQDs affects the crystal orientation of Bi2WO6.

Fig. 2b shows the Raman spectra of BWO, GQDs, and 16GQDs/BWO samples. No peak appears in the range of 1250–1650 cm−1 for the Raman spectrum of BWO. On the contrary, either GQDs or 16GQDs/BWO Raman spectra has a tiny peak at 1385 cm−1, corresponding to the D band of GQDs [38]. This result indicates the existence of GQDs in the sample.

Fig. 3 shows the XPS spectra of 10GQDs/BWO samples. Fig. 3a is the wide scanning spectrum, which reveals that C, O, Bi, and W elements can be detected on the surface of the samples; Fig. 3(b–e) exhibit the narrow scanning spectra of these four elements. All these spectra were calibrated by C 1s of aliphatic carbon at 284.8 eV. Fig. 3b illustrates the C–C bond with an sp2 orbital at 284.2 eV, which originates from the adventitious carbon [39]. The peaks of C–OH and sp2-hybridized carbon in C=O were detected at 285.8 eV and 287.7 eV , respectively. This indicates the existence of GQDs in the 10GQDs/BWO samples [40]. Fig. 3c presents that the O element spectrum of 10GQDs/BWO can be split into three peaks. The binding energy at 530.4 eV is attributed to the lattice oxygen in the BWO, while that at 529.4 eV corresponds to adsorbed oxygen [41]. Furthermore, there is an extra binding energy at 531.6 eV, which is due to the O–C/O–H bonds in GQDs, indicating the existence of GQDs [40]. The GQDs are chemically combined with the surface of Bi2WO6, functionalizing the edge sites with oxygen-containing groups [42]. In Fig. 3d, the peaks with binding energy at 158.6 eV and 163.9 eV correspond to Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 of the Bi3+ ions [43], respectively. The peaks with binding energies at 34.9 eV and 37.0 eV shown in Fig. 3e correspond to W 4f5/2 and W 4f7/2 of the W6+ ions in BWO [43], respectively. In summary, the spectra of C and O indicate the coexistence of GQDs and Bi2WO6 in the 10GQDs/BWO samples. This finding is supported by previous studies on GQDs composition with C3N4 [44], which shows that only the peaks of O and C changed after composition.

Fig. 4 a, b, c, and d show the FE-SEM images of BWO, 3GQDs/BWO, 10GQDs/BWO, and 16GQDs/BWO, respectively. It can be seen that GQDs affect the morphology of the composite material. Fig. 4a illustrates that the BWO samples have irregular structures and that a small number of 2D flakes tend to form 3D flowers. With increasing addition of GQDs, more and more nanosheets participate in the formation of 3D flower shape. Almost all 10GQDs/BWO and 16GQDs/BWO nanosheets are presented in the form of 3D aggregate. Fig. 4c also shows that 10GQDs/BWO samples have the most ideal 3D flower shape. Therefore, an appropriate portion of GQDs is critical for the composite to achieving favorable morphology.

Fig. 4f shows the EDS elemental mapping of C for the FE-SEM image of 10GQDs/BWO sample in Fig. 4e. Fig. 4f clearly shows the presence of GQDs, which concurs the XPS results. In addition, the C mapping confirms that uniform spatial distribution of the sample GQDs on the surface of Bi2WO6. This finding is further elaborated in the TEM results.

The BWO and 10GQDs/BWO samples characterized by TEM and HR-TEM further confirm the microstructure of the catalysts. Fig. 4g shows the low-magnification TEM images of 10GQDs/BWO materials, and the GQDs having about 5 nm in diameter are evenly deposited on BWO nanosheets, corresponding to the EDS carbon mapping shown in Fig. 4f. Fig. 4h shows the high-resolution TEM image of 10GQDs/BWO. The lattice interplanar spacing of 0.315 nm of the large nanosheets corresponds to the (131) plane of BWO [45], whereas the lattice interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm belongs to GQDs [46].

Fig. S3 in the Supporting Information (SI) shows the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of BWO and 10GQDs/BWO samples. The type IV isotherms of BWO and 10GQDs/BWO indicates the existence of mesopores in the samples [47]. The pore width of these two catalysts is in the range of 2.5–10 nm. However, the average pore width of 10GQDs/BWO (3.405 nm) is larger than that of BWO (3.055 nm). The calculated specific surface areas of BWO and 10GQDs/BWO are 19.55 m2 g−1 and 30.654 m2 g−1, respectively. These results prove that the specific surface area of Bi2WO6 can be increased by adding GQDs.

Fig. 5 a shows the optical absorption characteristics of the catalyst samples. All samples are responsive to visible lights (>380 nm). Compared to BWO, the absorption of GQDs/BWO catalysts slightly shifts to red, and great absorption intensity [32]. The sunlight absorption capacity of 10GQDs/BWO is the strongest among these photocatalysts. As shown in the Fig. 5b, the band gaps of the samples can be calculated by K-M method [48]. The band gap of BWO is 2.80 eV, which is consistent with previous research results [49]. The corresponding values for 3GQDs/BWO, 10GQDs/BWO, and 16GQDs/BWO are 2.78, 2.70, and 2.75 eV, respectively. This result confirms that introducing an appropriate content of GQDs can narrow the band gaps of the catalysts.

Fig. 6 shows the NO conversion rate of 10GQDs/BWO within 30 min under different light intensities. When light intensity is between 20 and 70 mW/cm2, the light intensity has a great impact on the conversion rate of catalyst for NO degradation. When it exceeds 70 mW/cm2, especially between 90 and 110 mW/cm2, its effect becomes insignificant, which indicates that the efficiency of light usage of the prepared catalyst is restricted. The excitation and the density of photo-generated carriers increase with increasing light intensity [50]. Photocatalyst can take full advantage of the excited carriers in the low light intensity region. However, when the light intensity is excessive, the recombination of h+ and e¯ is also promoted [51]. More carriers are recombined before being used at greater intensity. As a result, the conversion rate of photocatalytic degradation of NO increases insignificantly after light intensity reaches 70 mW/cm2.

Although the performance of GQDs/Bi2WO6 composite increases with increasing light intensity, we choose the light intensity of 25 mW/cm2 by considering both performance and actual practice conditions. First, the prepared catalyst showed a better performance under the lower intensity of 25 mW/cm2 than its competitors did at a greater intensity. Table 1 compares the performances between different photocatalysts. Second, the light intensity of 25 mW/cm2 is deemed practical. We measured the outdoor daylight intensity with a light intensity meter, and the light intensity was approximately 28 mW/cm2 at 11 am on January 24, 2021. Experimental results under this light intensity is a good reference for real applications.

Fig. 7 a shows the photocatalytic NO conversion rates of different catalysts under the simulated sunlight (25 mW/cm2). The NO conversion rate rises first and then decreases with increasing loading of GQDs. The 10GQDs/BWO composite achieves the optimum results. Its NO conversion rate is 73% during 30 min of simulated sunlight irradiation, which is 3.84 times higher than that of BWO sample. The photocatalytic degradation of NO with time can be referred to Fig. S4a. As for the BWO samples, the degradation efficiency of NO decreases gradually from 45% at the beginning to 12% in 30 min. When it comes to10GQDs/BWO composite, >95% of NO is removed at the initial reaction stage to 63% in 30 min. It indicates that GQDs can improve the ability of composite catalysts to degrade NO.

Furthermore, the NO conversion rate of 3GQDs/BWO and 16GQDs/BWO reaches 36% and 67% during 30 min, respectively, which are 1.89 and 3.53 times higher than BWO. The lower degradation efficiency of 3GQDs/BWO than those of 10GQDs/BWO and 16GQDs/BWO may be due to insufficient doping of GQDs on BWO surface. On the contrary, too much doping of GQDs also reduces catalytic performance because excessive GQDs are likely to block the sunlight absorption [52]. Moreover, comparison between Fig. 4c and Fig. 4d shows that excessive GQDs reduces the number of layers of flower-like catalyst. All this results in reduced adsorption of NO and photocatalytic active sites.

Fig. S5 shows the measurements of NOx for a typical test, in which

is always lower than the inlet NO concentration, which means that nitrogen element is not conserved in the gas. It indicates that the NO removed by photocatalysis produces not only NO2, but also other N-containing species.

Primary products of catalytic oxidation of NO are NOx − (x = 2 or 3) and NO2. Therefore, the knowledge on NO2 production and the types of anions generated by photocatalysis is crucial to the understanding of the mechanism of and nitrogen selectivity of NO photocatalytic oxidation. Fig. S4b shows the concentration change of NO2 at the outlet, the NO2 concentration at the outlet is less than 2.5 ppm for all photocatalysis tests, which lower than the amount of the oxidized NO. This difference in nitrogen elements indicates that NO2 is not the only oxidation product, and partial NO is likely oxidized into NOx − ions in this research. The IC result indicates that all the anion generated in the catalytic process is in the form of NO2 − ions. Therefore, we produce Fig. 8 is produced to show the chain of nitrogen in the photocatalytic process for different catalysts. The ratio of unremoved NO to the total NO input is given by

From Fig. 7b and Fig. 7c, the
S
N
O x -
and DeNOx index values of 3GQDs/BWO are reduced compared to BWO. However, 10GQDs/BWO and 16GQDs/BWO significantly improve these two indicators significantly. The
S
N
O x -
increases from 66 to 88%, and DeNOx index value rises from −0.003 to 0.43 for 16GQDs/BWO. It means that appropriate incorporation of GQDs to BWO is essential. Although the formation of NOx − ions can avoid polluting the environment again, these ions can occupy the active sites of the photocatalyst, causing the deactivation of catalyst [53]. This is why 16GQDs/BWO has higher selectivity, but its NO conversion rate is lower than 10GQDs/BWO. Related mechanisms will be elaborated later.

Fig. 7d shows the stability test results. Overall, the photocatalytic efficiency only reduced slightly after five cycles of NO degradation using 10GQDs/BWO, which indicated that the prepared GQDs/BWO could be used as an efficient and stable photocatalyst for the degradation of NO.

Table 1 compares our results with others’ in terms of the photocatalytic degradation of NO within 30 min of irradiation. Note that the light intensity used in this study is lower than those used by Li et al. [54] and Chang et al. [55]. Furthermore, the inlet NO concentration in our test is around 20 times higher instead; however, our samples achieved a higher degradation efficiency. All of these point to one conclusion that our as-prepared GQDs/BWO shows a superior catalytic performance.

Fig. 9 a shows the EIS test results that quantify the effects of GQDs on the charge carrier transfer and the separation efficiency of GQDs/BWO catalysts. The diameter of the arc of the EIS Nyquist plot represents the charge transfer rate that occurs at the contact interface between the electrolyte solution and the working electrode. The smaller the diameter means the better the performance of the samples [62]. The Nyquist-plot diameter of 10GQDs/BWO is smaller than that of BWO, which indicates that 10GQDs/BWO has a smaller charge transport resistance than BWO does. Therefore, the addition of GQDs enhances the transmission of photogenerated carriers on the interface of GQDs and Bi2WO6.

Fig. 9b shows the photocurrent density of the catalysts. The photocurrent densities of all GQDs/BWO catalysts are much higher than those of BWO when both are irradiated by simulated sunlight. As indicated by the blue line, 10GQDs/BWO has the highest photocurrent density; this further explains its best photocatalytic performance. The DFT calculations reveal that there is an electron flow from BWO to GQDs in the GQDs/BWO composites, revealing the good mobility of our fabricated GQDs. Therefore, the increase in photocurrent density may be due to the higher electron mobility of GQDs, which accelerates the separation of electron and hole [29,30]. All in all, the inclusion of GQDs can improve the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs of samples.

Fig. 10 shows the PL spectra of BWO and 10GQDs/BWO, which are of nearly the same shape. Both spectra have peaks at 468 nm when the excitation wavelength is 320 nm. However, the peak intensities of the two samples are different. The emission intensity of BWO is stronger than that of 10GQDs/BWO because the addition of GQDs greatly inhibits the recombination of electrons and holes [37]. This finding is consistent with the conclusion of EIS analysis.

Fig. 11 shows the results for active-species scavenger test using 10GQDs/BWO. The addition of K2Cr2O7, KI, and PBQ reduces the degradation efficiency of NO because e− , h+ , and
·
O 2 -
play important roles in photocatalysis [37]. Specifically, catalysts with the addition of K2Cr2O7 and KI both lose its ability to degrade NO. Moreover, from Fig. S6, NO concentration decreases rapidly and the production of NO2 is insignificant when PBQ is added, which indicates that
·
O 2 -
plays a role in the transformation of NO into NO2 as described in Eq. (7).

Furthermore, the efficiency of NO degradation increases after the addition of TBA, and more NO2 is generated (Fig. S6). It indicates that the
· O H
has slightly impact on NO removal. This is probably because
· O H
plays a role in the conversion of NO2 to NO2 − as described in Eq. (10), and the accumulation of the NO2 − ions on active sites reduces the removal of NO.

The MS plots are used to determine the conduction bands (CB) of BWO and 10GQDs/BWO. Fig. 12 shows the MS plots of BWO and 10GQDs/BWO. The positive slopes of the MS curves indicate that these samples belong to n-type semiconductors. The flat band (Efb ) potentials for BWO and 10GQDs/BWO are 0.39 and −0.72 vs. SCE, which are equivalent to 0.632 and − 0.478 V vs. NHE (normal hydrogen electrode), respectively, because E(NHE) = E(SCE) + 0.242 V. The CB edges of BWO and 10GQDs/BWO are 0.532 and − 0.578 V vs. NHE, respectively, because ECB = Efb – 0.1 V [63]. Combining the results of UV-vis DRS in Fig. 5b, the valence band (VB) potentials of BWO and 10GQDs/BWO are calculated as 3.332 and 2.122 V, respectively.

The preceding results indicate that the introduction of GQDs into BWO greatly changes the band structure. Despite the close band gaps, the 10GQDs/BWO composite has lower VB and CB potentials than BWO does. GQD material has a non-zero bandgap. Its CB and VB edges were reported to be −0.5 V and 0.9 V, respectively [45]. It can be seen that the CB edge of GQDs is very close to that of 10GQDs/BWO samples (-0.578 V). The VB potential of GQDs is higher than the CB potential of BWO (0.532 eV, vs. NHE). The e− on the CB of Bi2WO6 can be transferred to the VB of GQDs because of the high electron mobility of GQDs [29,64]. A Z-scheme heterojunction is probably formed between GQDs and Bi2WO6, allowing e− to flow from Bi2WO6 to combine with photogenerated holes in GQDs. The DFT calculations confirm the e− transfer from BWO to GQDs.

The DFT results show that the adsorption energies of sites were calculated after the optimization of GQDs/BWO, and the value at Position 1 (−2.05497 eV) < Position 2 (−0.87027 eV). This result indicated that Position 1 was more stable than Position 2. See Figs. S1c and S1e for the optimized structures of Position 1.

Fig. 13 a and b show the electron density difference isosurface. Positive values are in yellow, and negative ones are in blue. For positive electron density difference, the charge of the region increases with GQDs adsorption. The negative values represent area of charge reduction. Fig. 13 describes the charge rearrangement of GQDs after adsorption onto BWO. Bader charge calculation shows that GQDs obtained 0.004977 electrons. It indicates that there may be electrostatic adsorption between GQDs and BWO surfaces, and the GQDs receive electrons during the process of adsorption onto BWO surface. This is consistent with the enhancement of electron mobility by GQDs in the photocurrent response experiment.

As shown in Fig. 14 a and c, the VB top of BWO supercell is mainly composed of O 2p, Bi 6s, W 5d, and Bi 6p orbitals. Near the top of the valence band, the overlapping positions of the density-of-state (DOS) peaks of Bi 6S and O 2p indicate the hybridization of Bi 6S and O 2p orbitals. This is a common hybridization phenomenon in bismuth-based semiconductors [65,66]. The bottom of conduction band (CB) is composed of W 5d, O2p, and Bi 6p orbitals. This composition leads to the transition of photogenerated electrons to the W 5d orbital in the conduction band. For GQDs/BWO, the top of VB is mainly composed of O 2p, Bi 6p, and W 5d orbitals. At the bottom of CB, the slight addition of C 2p and 2S orbitals leads to a slight reduction of the band gap and enables the e − at the top of the VB to transfer to the empty C 2p orbitals. This is consistent with the gain of e − on the VB of GQDs in MS results.

The mechanism behind NO degradation using GQDs/BWO composite photocatalysts can be explained from electrochemical viewpoint. The MS plots show that the CB potential of BWO is 0.532 eV vs. NHE, which is higher than the
O 2
/
·
O 2 -
potential (−0.33 eV vs. NHE) [67]; therefore, the electrons on it cannot reduce O2 to
·
O 2 -
. However, the CB potential of GQDs (−0.578 eV vs. NHE) is sufficient to reduce O2 to
·
O 2 -
following Eq. (6). Additionally, the active species scavenger test shows that
·
O 2 -
is an effective and active substance in the catalytic oxidation of NO. Therefore, it reflects that the e− transfers from the CB of Bi2WO6 to the VB of GQDs. Such information provides reliable evidence for the establishment of an electron channel direction from Bi2WO6 to GQDs (see Fig. 15 ). As a result, the Z-scheme heterojunction is formed between Bi2WO6 and GQDs.

The finding that these channels can transfer the e− produced by Bi2WO6 to GQDs is consistent with DFT calculations, which confirm that the GQDs receive electrons during adsorption onto BWO surface. Hence, the reduction of O2 occurs on the GQDs. The VB potential of 10GQDs/BWO is 2.122 eV, thus, the holes on the VB potential can directly oxidize NO to NO2 − [68] (see Eq. (5)). In addition, it also can oxidize OH− to
· OH
(OH− /
· O H
, 1.9 eV vs. NHE) [67], as shown in Eq. (9). Eqs. (4)–(10) summarize the preceding analyses on reaction mechanisms.

In general, e− , h+ , and
·
O 2 -
species play important roles in the oxidation of NO, and
· O H
radical plays a role in the oxidation of NO2 to NO2 −. Based on the analysis of photocatalytic activity test and the active species detection experiments, the mechanism of photocatalytic oxidation of NO by GQDs/BWO as illustrated in Fig. 15 can be inferred.

GQDs/BWO composites were prepared by CA pyrolysis combined with hydrothermal method. The XRD patterns indicated that the presence of GQDs affected the crystal orientation of Bi2WO6. The Raman, XPS, EDS and HR-TEM results all proved that GQDs were successfully loaded onto the Bi2WO6 nanoparticles. The BET analysis showed that the inclusion of GQDs increased the specific surface area of the catalyst, and would provide more reactive sites. Moreover, the UV-vis DRS, PL, EIS and photocurrent density tests proved that the presence of GQDs could not only enhance light absorption, but also promoted the separation of electron-hole pairs and the conductivity of catalyst of GQDs/BWO. It could be due to the fact that GQDs were evenly deposited onto the surface of Bi2WO6, establishing photo-generated electron channels. More importantly, active species scavenger tests, MS tests, and DFT calculations verify the formation of Z-scheme between GQDs and Bi2WO6. These composites enhanced the degradation of NO at ppm-level under simulated sunlight irradiation. The maximum conversion rate of NO for 10GQDs/BWO achieved 73% during 30 min of irradiation, and the best selectivity for the formation of NOx -and DeNOx index are 88% and 0.43 for 16GQDs/BWO. This catalytic performance was superior to that of existing competitors. Finally, the stability of photocatalyst was verified cyclic experiments, indicating that it has promising commercial value.
